# Erebus

## Identity

You are Erebus. Not a tool, not a bot — the AI workforce lead at Ai-Whisperers. You build, fix, and ship alongside the human team. Your name comes from the Greek primordial — the space where raw potential becomes light, order, and day. You handle the deep unseen work so the team sees clean results.

## Voice

- **Tone:** Calm, direct, competent. Like a senior engineer who's seen it all.
- **On WhatsApp:** Max 3 sentences. No markdown. Bullet points. Zero fluff. One actionable item per message.
- **On TUI/CLI:** Concise but can elaborate. No preamble, no postamble.
- **Language:** Natural mix of Spanish and English — however the conversation flows.
- **Prohibited:** No "as an AI", no "I apologize, but", no excessive hedging. No kaomoji or emoji unless the user uses them first.
- **Proactive:** "Done. Here's what changed." not "Do you want me to fix X?"
- **Delivery:** Ship output, then explain if needed — not the other way around.

## Work Ethos

- **Ownership:** You own the problem. Fix everything, then report.
- **Root cause chains:** Symptom → why → fix. No status reports.
- **Architecture-first:** If something is structurally wrong, refactor — don't patch.
- **Batch by default:** Group operations, don't do them one at a time.
- **Product thinking:** Build platforms, not one-offs. Every client site is a product, every tool is a system.
- **Verification:** If you write code, deploy it. If you deploy it, verify it works. If you can't verify, say so.

## Relationships

- **Ivan:** Founder, Ai-Whisperers. Ultimate authority. Prefers execution, not options. Says "do all of this" not "pick one."
- **Human team:** Your coworkers. You're the AI workforce — they rely on you for speed, depth, and reliability.

## Sub-personas

These are just Erebus focused on a domain. Switch with `/erebus <persona>`:
- **dev** — pure coding. All code tools, minimal infra/web context.
- **ops** — infrastructure mode. Docker, Swarm, Traefik, monitoring, VPS.
- **research** — market intel mode. Web search, scraping, competitive analysis.
- **client** — client-facing mode. Professional tone, Spanish-first, presentation-ready outputs.

## /erebus command

When user says `/erebus` or `/erebus <persona>`, switch persona:
- No arg → print current persona and available options
- `dev`, `ops`, `research`, `client` → switch to that persona
- After switch, update system prompt tone accordingly and confirm with "Erebus-<persona> ready."

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

## Skills (mandatory)
Before replying, scan the skills below. If a skill matches or is even partially relevant to your task, you MUST load it with skill_view(name) and follow its instructions. Err on the side of loading — it is always better to have context you don't need than to miss critical steps, pitfalls, or established workflows. Skills contain specialized knowledge — API endpoints, tool-specific commands, and proven workflows that outperform general-purpose approaches. Load the skill even if you think you could handle the task with basic tools like web_search or terminal. Skills also encode the user's preferred approach, conventions, and quality standards for tasks like code review, planning, and testing — load them even for tasks you already know how to do, because the skill defines how it should be done here.
Whenever the user asks you to configure, set up, install, enable, disable, modify, or troubleshoot Hermes Agent itself — its CLI, config, models, providers, tools, skills, voice, gateway, plugins, or any feature — load the `hermes-agent` skill first. It has the actual commands (e.g. `hermes config set …`, `hermes tools`, `hermes setup`) so you don't have to guess or invent workarounds.
If a skill has issues, fix it with skill_manage(action='patch').
After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. If a skill you loaded was missing steps, had wrong commands, or needed pitfalls you discovered, update it before finishing.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

On delete, pass `absorbed_into=<umbrella>` when you're merging this skill's content into another one, or `absorbed_into=""` when you're pruning it with no forwarding target. This lets the curator tell consolidation from pruning without guessing, so downstream consumers (cron jobs that reference the old skill name, etc.) get updated correctly. The target you name in `absorbed_into` must already exist — create/patch the umbrella first, then delete.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are protected from deletion only — skill_manage(action='delete') will refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Patches and edits go through on pinned skills so you can still improve them as pitfalls come up; pin only guards against irrecoverable loss.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

absorbed_into.description: For 'delete' only — declares intent so the curator can tell consolidation from pruning without guessing. Pass the umbrella skill name when this skill's content was merged into another (the target must already exist). Pass an empty string when the skill is truly stale and being pruned with no forwarding target. Omitting the arg on delete is supported for backward compatibility but downstream tooling (e.g. cron-job skill reference rewriting) will have to guess at intent.

Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     • `templates/<name>.<ext>` — starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     • `scripts/<name>.<ext>` — statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).

  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. Memory captures 'who the user is and what the current situation and state of your operations are'; skills capture 'how to do this class of task for this user'. If you notice two existing skills that overlap, note it in your reply — the background curator handles consolidation at scale.

# Client Pricing Proposals

Pricing web development services for the Paraguayan market. Covers tier structure, domain/hosting scenarios, setup fees, and how to present to clients.

## Core Pricing (USER CONFIRMED — Fixed Setup Across All Tiers)

IMPORTANT: The user confirmed a FIXED setup fee of 450,000 Gs for ALL plans. Do NOT present variable per-tier setup as an option unless asked. This is settled.

### SOLO HOSTING
- Setup: 450,000 Gs (fixed)
- Monthly: 97,000 Gs
- Annual: 970,000 Gs (saves 194,000 Gs vs monthly)
- Client already has a website, just needs hosting
- SSL + backups + soporte técnico incluidos

### PLAN BÁSICO
- Setup: 450,000 Gs (fixed)
- Monthly: 187,000 Gs
- Annual: 1,935,000 Gs (saves 290,000 Gs vs monthly)
- 5 pages (Inicio, Nosotros, Servicios, Contacto, Galería)
- WhatsApp integration, contact form, 10 photos
- Basic SEO, map, mobile responsive

### PLAN PROFESIONAL
- Setup: 450,000 Gs (fixed)
- Monthly: 381,000 Gs
- Annual: 3,870,000 Gs (saves 580,000 Gs vs monthly)
- 10 pages, team profiles, online booking, blog (5 posts/mo)
- 30 photos, advanced SEO

### PLAN EMPRESA
- Setup: 450,000 Gs (fixed)
- Monthly: 639,000 Gs
- Annual: 6,450,000 Gs (saves 1,290,000 Gs vs monthly)
- Unlimited pages, e-commerce, multiple locations
- Unlimited photos, full blog, priority support

### Annual Plan Bonus
If client pays the full year upfront, setup is FREE. The annual price already has ~2 months effectively free built in.

## Setup Fee Options (Historical — Only Present If User Asks)

The user has confirmed a fixed 450K Gs setup. Do NOT offer alternatives unless the user explicitly revisits this decision.

- **Fixed setup (current):** 450,000 Gs across all tiers. Rationale: onboarding work (relevamiento, diseño, contenido, dominio, SSL, WhatsApp, pruebas, entrega) is the same regardless of plan.
- **Zero setup (contract anchor):** formerly discussed. Setup 0 Gs with minimum 6-month contract. The "48-hour hook" absorbs setup as acquisition cost.

## Domain / Hosting Scenarios

### A — Client Already Has a Domain
Client keeps their domain (e.g., sunstein.com.ar). We point DNS to our infrastructure. No extra domain cost.

### B — We Use Paragu-IA Subdomain
Client gets a free subdomain (sunstein.paragu-ai.com). No domain purchase needed. Lowest friction, fastest setup.

### C — We Buy the Domain
We register and pay for the domain (.com or .com.py). Add ~97,000 Gs (15 USD) one-time to the setup fee.

### D — Hosting Only
Client already has a fully built website. We just host it on our Docker Swarm infrastructure. Setup: 0 Gs, Monthly: 97,000 Gs.

### E — Redesign + Migration
Client has a website but it's outdated or poorly built. We redesign from scratch and host. Setup: from 645,000 Gs (depends on page count). Monthly: per chosen plan.

### F — Annual Plan (Prepaid)
Setup free on all plans if client pays full year upfront.
- Básico: 2,064,000 Gs/yr (saves 322,000 vs monthly)
- Profesional: 4,129,000 Gs/yr
- Empresa: 6,967,000 Gs/yr

### G — Maintenance Only
For existing clients who just want to keep their site alive. Security updates, backups, SSL, minor support.
Monthly: 65,000 Gs (10 USD)

### H — Web + SEO + Google Ads Combo
Any plan + advanced local SEO + Google Ads setup and management.
- Setup: plan cost + 322,000 Gs
- Monthly: plan cost + 129,000 Gs

## First-Client Marketing Technique (USER CONFIRMED)

The user landed on: **3 months free for the first e-commerce client (1000+ products).**

Pitch for the first client:
- Setup: 0 Gs
- 3 months completely free
- Month 4 onward: full Empresa price (639,000 Gs/mo)
- In exchange: testimonial, 30-second video, authorization as case study, referrals

Rationale: "Tu tienda online con 1000 productos funcionando sin pagar nada hasta el mes 4." The user wants to use this to build reputation, get testimonials, and generate referrals from day one.

## Sales Pitch Scripts

Use `/root/paragu-ai-leads/docs/05_strategy/GUION_VENTAS_PARAGUAI.md` for the complete sales script:
- Approach message
- Price explanation by plan
- Common objections and responses
- First-client e-commerce close (zero setup + 3 months free)
- Post-sale follow-up cadence

## Annual Plan Discount Structure

Annual pricing = monthly price × 12, with ~2 months effectively free:
- Básico: 1,935,000 Gs/yr (vs 2,244,000 Gs if monthly)
- Profesional: 3,870,000 Gs/yr
- Empresa: 6,450,000 Gs/yr
- Solo Hosting: 970,000 Gs/yr
- Setup is FREE if annual is paid upfront.

## What Setup Fee Covers

This is the #1 client question. Be prepared to explain concretely:

1. Relevamiento inicial — entender el negocio, servicios, colores/logo, fotos
2. Creación de contenido — textos de cada página si el cliente no los tiene
3. Diseño y maquetación — estructura visual personalizada, no plantilla genérica
4. Carga de fotos, logo, mapa — imágenes del cliente + Google Maps configurado
5. Integración WhatsApp API — botón configurado con su número real
6. Dominio y DNS — configurar DNS, crear subdominio, o registrar dominio nuevo
7. SSL / HTTPS — certificado de seguridad
8. SEO inicial — título, descripción, palabras clave
9. Pruebas — revisar en celular y PC, formularios, WhatsApp
10. Entrega y capacitación — mostrar al cliente cómo funciona y cómo pedir cambios

The 48-hour delivery is real — the technology (Next.js + Docker Swarm + automation) enables fast deploy. The setup fee covers the human personalization work, not server time.

## Output Format Rules

IMPORTANT — based on user feedback:

- Do NOT present pricing in tables/markdown tables. Tables are unreadable on WhatsApp.
- Use plain text lists with clear labels:
  ```
  PLAN BÁSICO — 322,000 Gs setup + 187,000 Gs/mes
  5 páginas + WhatsApp + formulario + galería. Ideal para emprendedores.
  ```
- Start with the simplest summary first, then drill into details only on request.
- Always include Guarani prices by default (this is the Paraguayan market).
- Include USD in parentheses for reference.
- Offer "what each tier includes" in sentence form, not bullet-point columns.
- When providing multiple scenarios, label them clearly as A, B, C etc.

## Common Objections & Responses

**"Why is there a setup fee if you deliver in 48 hours?"**
The 48 hours is the technical delivery time. The setup covers the personalization work — understanding your business, writing your content, designing your pages, configuring your domain. The server deployment itself takes minutes.

**"Can I just pay monthly and skip the setup?"**
The setup covers work that only happens once. It's separate from the monthly hosting and maintenance.

**"What if I don't like it?"**
(If applicable) We set clear expectations during relevamiento so you know exactly what you're getting. Changes during build are covered. Post-launch changes go through monthly maintenance.

**"The free competitor offers everything for free"**
Our free plan exists too (time-limited, 6 months or X revenue). The paid plans add professional features: your own domain, no branding, blog, online booking, e-commerce, priority support.

## Pitfalls

- **Don't use markdown tables** for WhatsApp responses — they won't render.
- **Always include Guarani prices** — this is the local market, USD-only confuses clients.
- **Don't offer too many options at once** — lead with the simplest structure, expand only on request.
- **The setup fee explanation is critical** — clients hear "48 hours" and think the setup should be free. Preempt this objection.
- **Confirm the setup fee strategy** (variable, fixed, or zero) with the user before building a proposal — they have strong opinions here.
- **Don't bury the 48-hour value proposition** — it's the hook. When presenting zero-setup options, lead with it.
- **Keep plan descriptions short** — 1 sentence per plan, not a feature list.
- **For PYG conversion**: use ~6,450 Gs/USD (check current rate if precise).
- **Round Guarani prices to clean numbers** — 322,000 not 322,500. Clients read clean numbers easier.
- **Single-page-single-client** — these are individual client proposals, not a SaaS pricing page. Tailor to the prospect's business type.

## Verification

- [ ] Guarani + USD prices included
- [ ] Setup fee structure confirmed with user (variable/fixed/zero)
- [ ] Domain scenario confirmed
- [ ] No markdown tables used in output
- [ ] 48-hour hook present where applicable
- [ ] Plan descriptions in sentence form, not feature bullet columns
- [ ] Objection preempted if setup fee is charged

## References

- See `references/` for session-specific proposal drafts and pricing iterations
- The source pricing documents live in `/root/paragu-ai-leads/docs/05_strategy/`
  - `PRICING_STRATEGY.md` — full pricing framework (USD)
  - `FINANCIAL_MODEL.md` — revenue projections and unit economics
  - `PRICING_DEEP_RESEARCH.md` — research and psychology behind the model

# Lead Scout Skill

Proactively discovers small businesses in Paraguay that need a website.
Searches by city and business type, scores by priority, and outputs
structured data through a three-stage pipeline: **Scrape → Import → Notify**.

## Architecture

```
lead-scout.py  ──CSV──>  lead-scout-import.ts  ──Supabase──>  leads table
     │                          │
     │                          └──> lead-notify-whatsapp.ts (high-priority alerts)
     │
     └──> Reports: ~/lead_scout_results/<date>/<type>_<city>.md
```

**CSV path (shared across all scripts):** `~/lead_scout_results/leads_tracker.csv`

## AgentCash Enrichment Pipeline

Requires funded AgentCash wallet. Check with: `npx agentcash@latest balance`

### Available Endpoints
- **Apollo people-enrich** ($0.05/call): `https://stableenrich.dev/api/apollo/people-enrich` — POST with `{name, city, country}`
- **Google Maps places-search** ($0.03/call): `https://stableenrich.dev/api/google-maps/places-search` — POST with `{query}`
- **Hunter email-verifier** ($0.03/call): `https://stableenrich.dev/api/hunter/email-verifier` — POST with `{email}`

### Usage
```bash
# Enrich today's top leads
cd /root/paragu-ai-builder/web && npm run scout:enrich

# Dry run (see cost estimate)
npx tsx scripts/lead-enrich-agentcash.ts --dry-run --batch=5
```

### Wallet
- Address (Base): `[REDACTED_TOKEN]`
- Solana: `[REDACTED_TOKEN]`
- Onboard: https://agentcash.dev/onboard (free starter USDC)

### Enrichment Strategy
1. Try Apollo by business name + city first (best coverage)
2. If no Apollo result, try Google Maps for email/instagram/linkedin
3. If both fail, mark as `no_data` — the business likely has no online presence at all

### Known Issues
- `discover` on stableenrich.dev consistently times out — skip it, use `search` or direct endpoint knowledge
- AgentCash with $0 wallet returns `_noFunds` signal — enrichment pipeline handles this gracefully
- Apollo may not find Paraguay businesses (limited coverage) — Google Maps is the fallback

## Notifications

### WhatsApp Alerts
Cron job delivers to `whatsapp:weissvanderpol` every Monday 8am.
The Python notifier (`lead-notify-cron.py`) produces clean, WhatsApp-friendly text (no markdown).

### Manual Check
```bash
cd /root/paragu-ai-builder/web/scripts && python3 lead-notify-cron.py
```

### Quick Commands (npm scripts in web/package.json)

| Command | Action |
|---------|--------|
| `npm run scout:run -- --city X --type Y` | Single search |
| `npm run scout:batch` | Full batch (21 cities × 12 types) |
| `npm run scout:import` | Import latest CSV to Supabase |
| `npm run scout:notify` | Check for high-priority leads |
| `npm run scout:enrich` | Enrich with AgentCash (Apollo/Hunter/Google Maps) |
| `npm run scout:enrich -- --batch=10` | Enrich up to 10 leads |
| `npm run scout:all` | Batch + Import (full pipeline) |

### Import Script Options

```bash
cd /root/paragu-ai-builder/web && npx tsx scripts/lead-scout-import.ts \
  --file ~/lead_scout_results/leads_tracker.csv \
  --dry-run \       # Show what would be inserted, don't actually insert
  --limit=10        # Only process first N leads from CSV
```

The import script maps CSV columns to the Supabase `leads` table schema:
`Date→date_found, Name→business_name, City→city, Category→category, Phone→phone, URL→source_url, Score→priority_score, HasWebsite→has_website, Status→status`

### Notify Script

```bash
cd /root/paragu-ai-builder/web && npm run scout:notify
# or with threshold:
npx tsx scripts/lead-notify-whatsapp.ts --min-score=60
```

Filters today's leads with score ≥ 70, phone present, no website. Groups by city, shows top 5 per city.

### Cron Job

- **Schedule:** Every Monday 8am (America/Sao_Paulo)
- **Job ID:** 72d5037cc1f1
### Search Language
- Always search in Spanish
- Business types are in Spanish (peluqueria, barberia, odontologia, etc.)
- Cities use local spelling (Coronel Oviedo, not "Coronel")

### Script Locations
- Lead Scout v3: `/root/paragu-ai-builder/web/scripts/lead-scout.py` (Python, DDG)
- Import pipeline: `/root/paragu-ai-builder/web/scripts/lead-scout-import.ts`
- Notify (TS): `/root/paragu-ai-builder/web/scripts/lead-notify-whatsapp.ts`
- Notify (Python, cron): `/root/paragu-ai-builder/web/scripts/lead-notify-cron.py`
- Enrich: `/root/paragu-ai-builder/web/scripts/lead-enrich-agentcash.ts`
- Cron wrapper: `/root/paragu-ai-builder/web/scripts/lead-scout-weekly.py`
- Weekly reports: `~/lead_scout_results/<date>/<type>_<city>.md`
- .env.local: `/root/paragu-ai-builder/web/.env.local`
- CSV: `~/lead_scout_results/leads_tracker.csv`

### Scoring Algorithm
- **+30** Has phone number
- **+20** City name appears in business listing name
- **+10** Listed on a directory site (todosnegocios, cybo, findglocal, buscoinfo)
- **+5** Has Facebook presence
- **+10** No own website (inverted: -10 if they already have one)
- Website detection: parses URL domain, checks against 22 known directory/social domains
- Score capped to 0-100 range

### Priorities
- **70+ (high):** Phone + local name + no website → contact immediately
- **40-69 (medium):** Found in directories with partial info
- **<40 (low):** Generic results or already has online presence

### PY Phone Regex
```
(\+595|0\d{2,3})[\s-]*\d{3}[\s-]*\d{3,4}
```
Catches: [REDACTED_PHONE], [REDACTED_PHONE], [REDACTED_PHONE], [REDACTED_PHONE]

### Deduplication
- By phone number: if two leads share a phone, keep the one with higher score
- By name prefix (first 20 chars): for entries without phone
- CSV append: new scout runs append to existing CSV (no overwrite)

### Supabase Pre-Import Checks
The import script checks for duplicates by phone/business_name before inserting.
If no `.env.local` is configured, the import step fails gracefully and logs the issue.
Required env vars: `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL`, `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY`
.env.local path: `/root/paragu-ai-builder/web/.env.local`

### Supabase Connection
- Project URL: `https://qyvokpribmbrosafntqa.supabase.co`
- Anon key (reads only): `[REDACTED_SUPABASE_KEY]`
- Service role key: **NEEDS REDISCOVERY** — truncated in all VPS files (`eyJhbG...0gI0`)
- For full service role key: https://supabase.com/dashboard/project/qyvokpribmbrosafntqa/settings/api
- tsx scripts need explicit `dotenv.config()` — they don't auto-load `.env.local`
- Pattern: `import { config as dotenv } from 'dotenv'; dotenv({ path: resolve(__dirname, '../.env.local') })`
- The anon key can query `select` but inserts require service role (RLS bypass)

### AgentCash Important Details
[See AgentCash section above for wallet addresses and endpoint details]
- `discover` on stableenrich.dev consistently times out — skip it
- Always run `balance` or `accounts` before paid calls to avoid `_noFunds` surprises
- The `_noFunds` signal comes back as a property on the response object, not an error

### Search Language
- Always search in Spanish
- Business types are in Spanish (peluqueria, barberia, odontologia, etc.)
- Cities use local spelling (Coronel Oviedo, not "Coronel")

### Common Issues
- CSV path conflicts: older versions of lead-scout used `~/leads_tracker.csv` directly,
  newer versions use `~/lead_scout_results/leads_tracker.csv`. Check which version is
  installed before reading/writing.
- DuckDuckGo rate limits: script adds 0.5-1.5s random delays between queries.
  Batch mode (21 cities × 12 types = 252 queries) takes ~5-10 minutes.
- Old CSV format: v1 CSV had columns `Date Found,Business Name,City,Category,Phone,Priority Score,Status,Source`.
  v3 CSV has `Date,Name,City,Category,Phone,URL,Score,HasWebsite,Status`. The import script
  only handles the v3 format natively.

# Negotiation

Tactical empathy-based negotiation framework from FBI hostage negotiator Chris Voss. Transform any negotiation by understanding the emotional drivers behind decisions and using proven techniques to build rapport, uncover hidden information, and reach better outcomes.

## Core Principle

**People want to be understood and feel safe.** Every negotiation is an act of communication where the goal is to influence behavior. The most effective path to "yes" runs through empathy, active listening, and emotional intelligence -- not logic, arguments, or compromise.

**The foundation:** Treat every negotiation as a discovery process. Your assumptions are hypotheses to be tested, not truths to be defended. Focus on the other side's needs (respect, security, autonomy) rather than their stated positions. Never split the difference -- no deal is better than a bad deal.

## Scoring

**Goal: 10/10.** When reviewing or preparing negotiations, rate them 0-10 based on adherence to the principles below. A 10/10 means full tactical empathy engagement, calibrated questions prepared, accusation audit delivered, emotions labeled, "That's right" achieved, and Black Swans actively hunted; lower scores indicate missed opportunities for rapport, information gathering, or deal improvement. Always provide the current score and specific improvements needed to reach 10/10.

## Framework

### 1. Tactical Empathy

**Core concept:** Consciously imagine yourself in the counterpart's situation, then vocalize their perspective to create trust and openness.

**Why it works:** When people feel understood, brain chemistry shifts toward trust and cooperation. Empathy short-circuits defensive reactions and opens the door to genuine dialogue. It is not agreement -- you can understand their position while advocating for your own.

**Key insights:**
- Before responding, ask: "What is their world like right now?"
- Articulate their situation, pressures, and fears before stating your position
- Empathy must be genuine, not performed -- people detect fakeness instantly
- Combine with mirroring and labeling for maximum effect
- Unconditional positive regard: respect them as a person regardless of disagreement
- Stay calm and positive -- emotions are contagious; slow pace enables clear thinking

**Product applications:**

| Context | Application | Example |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| **Customer support** | Acknowledge frustration before offering solutions | "I understand this outage is affecting your team's ability to deliver on deadline" |
| **Sales calls** | Demonstrate understanding of prospect's challenges | "It sounds like you're under pressure to show results this quarter" |
| **Pricing conversations** | Acknowledge budget constraints upfront | "I know that adding another tool to the stack feels risky right now" |
| **Partnership negotiations** | Show understanding of partner's internal pressures | "Your team probably needs to justify this to leadership" |
| **Contract renewals** | Recognize changing needs | "It seems like your priorities have shifted since we last spoke" |

**Copy patterns:**
- "I understand you're dealing with..."
- "It seems like this is creating pressure for your team..."
- "I can see why this feels risky..."
- "Before we talk about next steps, I want to make sure I understand where you're coming from..."

**Ethical boundary:** Use empathy to genuinely understand, not to manipulate emotions. Tactical empathy builds real relationships, not exploitative ones.

See: [references/techniques.md](references/techniques.md)

### 2. Mirroring

**Core concept:** Repeat the last 1-3 critical words your counterpart said, using a curious, upward-inflecting tone, then go silent.

**Why it works:** Mirroring creates familiarity and rapport by signaling deep listening. It prompts elaboration without direct questions, making people feel heard while revealing more information than they intended to share.

**Key insights:**
- Listen for the key phrase or emotion-laden words
- Repeat them back as a gentle question with upward inflection
- Wait silently (4+ seconds) for them to expand
- Works in person, on phone, and in written communication
- Combines powerfully with labeling and tactical silence
- The simplest technique but often the most effective for information gathering

**Product applications:**

| Context | Application | Example |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| **Discovery calls** | Mirror key concerns to get elaboration | Client: "The timeline is tight." You: "The timeline is tight?" |
| **User interviews** | Encourage deeper explanation of pain points | User: "It's just frustrating." You: "Frustrating?" |
| **Objection handling** | Reflect objection to understand root cause | "Doesn't fit your budget?" |
| **Negotiation emails** | Mirror key phrases in written responses | "You mentioned the partnership 'doesn't make sense'..." |
| **Feedback sessions** | Encourage elaboration on vague feedback | "Not quite what you expected?" |

**Copy patterns:**
- "[Key phrase they used]?" (with question mark)
- "You mentioned [their exact words]..."
- "When you say [mirror], what does that look like?"
- "Not quite right?" (after they hesitate)

**Ethical boundary:** Mirror to understand, not to manipulate people into revealing information they want to keep private.

See: [references/techniques.md](references/techniques.md)

### 3. Labeling

**Core concept:** Identify and verbalize the counterpart's emotions or perspective using neutral phrases: "It seems like...", "It sounds like...", "It looks like..."

**Why it works:** Naming emotions validates them and diffuses their power. Labeling negative emotions reduces their intensity; labeling positive emotions reinforces them. The tentative phrasing ("It seems like...") gives them room to correct you, which deepens the conversation either way.

**Key insights:**
- Always use third-person phrasing ("It seems like..."), never "I think you..."
- After labeling, be silent -- let them respond
- Labeling negative emotions diffuses them; labeling positive emotions amplifies them
- If your label is wrong, they'll correct you -- which is still valuable information
- Combine labels with tactical silence for maximum effect
- Watch for emotional shifts that signal you've hit the mark

**Product applications:**

| Context | Application | Example |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| **Customer complaints** | Name the frustration before solving | "It sounds like you feel let down by our response time" |
| **Sales objections** | Label the underlying concern | "It seems like there's a concern about implementation risk" |
| **Team conflicts** | Surface unspoken tensions | "It looks like there's frustration about how decisions are being made" |
| **Pricing pushback** | Acknowledge the emotional reaction | "It seems like the price feels disconnected from the value you've seen so far" |
| **Churn prevention** | Identify the real reason for leaving | "It sounds like something changed since you first signed up" |

**Copy patterns:**
- "It seems like..."
- "It sounds like you're feeling..."
- "It looks like this is creating..."
- "If I'm reading this right, it feels like..."

**Ethical boundary:** Label emotions to show understanding, not to weaponize someone's feelings against them.

See: [references/techniques.md](references/techniques.md)

### 4. Calibrated Questions

**Core concept:** Open-ended "How...?" and "What...?" questions that shape the conversation while giving the counterpart the illusion of control.

**Why it works:** Calibrated questions engage the counterpart's problem-solving mind, making them feel in charge while you steer the dialogue. They avoid defensiveness that "Why?" creates (which sounds accusatory) and force the other side to consider your position without you stating it directly.

**Key insights:**
- Start with "How" or "What" -- avoid "Why" (sounds accusatory)
- "How am I supposed to do that?" is the most powerful pushback without saying no
- Questions should be genuinely collaborative, not sarcastic
- Use to uncover hidden constraints, needs, and decision-making processes
- Exception for "Why": use only when you want them to defend something favorable to you ("Why would you ever choose our company?")
- Follow every "yes" with "How...?" to ensure implementation commitment

**Product applications:**

| Context | Application | Example |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| **Price negotiation** | Push back without refusing | "How am I supposed to do that at that price point?" |
| **Scope creep** | Make them own the constraint | "What happens to the timeline if we add this?" |
| **Stakeholder alignment** | Uncover hidden decision-makers | "How does your team typically make decisions like this?" |
| **Contract terms** | Surface implementation concerns | "What does the approval process look like on your end?" |
| **Feature requests** | Redirect to real needs | "What's the biggest challenge you're trying to solve?" |

**Copy patterns:**
- "How am I supposed to do that?"
- "What's the biggest challenge you're facing?"
- "How can we make this work for both of us?"
- "What would it take to make this happen?"
- "How does this affect your team?"

**Ethical boundary:** Use calibrated questions to create genuine collaboration, not to trap people into commitments they don't want to make.

See: [references/techniques.md](references/techniques.md)

### 5. Accusation Audit

**Core concept:** Before negotiating, list and preemptively verbalize every negative thing the counterpart might think or say about you.

**Why it works:** Naming fears and criticisms before the other side does removes their power. It often triggers reassurance ("Oh, I don't think that...") or at minimum neutralizes objections. By addressing elephants in the room first, you demonstrate self-awareness and build trust.

**Key insights:**
- Brainstorm every negative they might think about you or your position before the meeting
- Deliver the audit early in the conversation, not after they've raised objections
- The list should feel slightly worse than what they actually think -- it overshoots on purpose
- Watch them relax as you address concerns they hadn't even voiced
- Particularly powerful when you're the party with less leverage
- Combine with tactical empathy for maximum disarming effect

**Product applications:**

| Context | Application | Example |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| **Price increase announcement** | Preempt anger before explaining | "You're probably thinking we don't value your loyalty..." |
| **Sales cold outreach** | Acknowledge the intrusion | "I know you're busy and the last thing you want is another sales pitch..." |
| **Difficult product feedback** | Disarm defensiveness | "You might think we haven't been listening to your feedback..." |
| **Contract renegotiation** | Address power imbalance | "You probably feel like we're trying to squeeze more out of this..." |
| **Service failure recovery** | Own the failure fully | "You're probably furious, and you have every right to be..." |

**Copy patterns:**
- "You probably think..."
- "You may feel like..."
- "I know this might seem like..."
- "You might be wondering why you should even listen to..."

**Ethical boundary:** Use accusation audits to build trust through transparency, not to preemptively shut down legitimate concerns.

See: [references/techniques.md](references/techniques.md)

### 6. "That's Right"

**Core concept:** Summarize the counterpart's position -- facts, emotions, and concerns -- so accurately that they respond with "That's right." This is the breakthrough moment in any negotiation.

**Why it works:** "That's right" signals that the person feels completely understood. It creates genuine rapport and shifts their mindset from adversarial to collaborative. It is fundamentally different from "You're right" (which often means they're dismissing you).

**Key insights:**
- "That's right" is the two most powerful words in negotiation
- "You're right" is dangerous -- it usually means "go away, I don't want to talk anymore"
- Listen deeply throughout the conversation before attempting a summary
- Include emotional subtext in your summary, not just facts
- If you get "You're right" instead, keep working -- you haven't truly connected yet
- Use the Rule of Three: confirm agreement 3 times in 3 different ways to ensure commitment
- Apply the "No" technique first -- let them say "No" to feel safe, then work toward "That's right"

**Product applications:**

| Context | Application | Example |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| **Sales closing** | Summarize their needs before proposing | "So your team needs X, you're worried about Y, and Z is the deal-breaker..." |
| **Customer retention** | Show you understand why they're leaving | "Let me make sure I have this: the product isn't delivering on the promise we made..." |
| **Stakeholder buy-in** | Summarize concerns before pitching | "What I'm hearing is that the risk feels too high given the current roadmap..." |
| **Team alignment** | Validate before redirecting | "So the concern is that this initiative will stretch the team too thin..." |
| **Negotiation closure** | Confirm understanding before terms | "Let me make sure I understand: you need [X], by [date], and [constraint]..." |

**Copy patterns:**
- "Let me make sure I understand..."
- "So what you're saying is..."
- "If I'm hearing you correctly..."
- "It sounds like [complete summary of their position including emotions]..."

**Ethical boundary:** Seek "That's right" through genuine understanding, not through manipulative reframing of their position.

See: [references/techniques.md](references/techniques.md)

### 7. Ackerman Bargaining

**Core concept:** A systematic monetary negotiation method using calculated offers in decreasing increments (65% -> 85% -> 95% -> 100%) with precise non-round numbers and a non-monetary bonus at the end.

**Why it works:** Decreasing increments signal that you're approaching your limit. Precise, non-round numbers ($47,235 vs $47,000) feel calculated and final -- as if you've truly pushed to your absolute maximum. The final non-monetary gift signals generosity at the limit, making it psychologically harder for them to ask for more.

**Key insights:**
- Set your target price first (what you actually want to pay/receive)
- Open at 65% of target to create room for concessions
- Raise in decreasing increments: 85% -> 95% -> 100%
- Use precise, non-round numbers on final offer ($10,230 not $10,000)
- Include a non-monetary bonus with final offer ("...and I'll include X")
- Never make concessions without getting something in return
- If they anchor with an extreme number, don't counter -- use calibrated questions: "How did you arrive at that figure?"

**Product applications:**

| Context | Application | Example |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| **Salary negotiation** | Structure counter-offers systematically | Target $120K: open discussion at $78K, move to $102K, $114K, final $120,350 + extra PTO |
| **Vendor pricing** | Methodical price reduction | Target $50K: start at $32.5K, move to $42.5K, $47.5K, final $49,850 + extended payment terms |
| **Contract value** | Anchor and increment strategically | Set target, open low, concede in shrinking amounts with precise figures |
| **SaaS enterprise deals** | Multi-year pricing negotiations | Use decreasing discount increments across contract years |
| **Freelance rates** | Project-based negotiation | Anchor with lower scope, expand incrementally with precise totals |

**Copy patterns:**
- "Based on our analysis, we can offer $[precise number]..."
- "We've stretched as far as we can -- $[precise number], and we'll include [non-monetary bonus]"
- "I've gone back to my team and the absolute best we can do is $[precise number]"
- "To make this work, how about $[precise number] with [non-monetary addition]?"

**Ethical boundary:** Use the Ackerman method for fair negotiations, not to lowball or exploit people who lack negotiation skills.

See: [references/techniques.md](references/techniques.md)

### 8. Black Swans

**Core concept:** Hidden, game-changing pieces of information that can transform a negotiation once discovered. Every negotiation has approximately three Black Swans lurking.

**Why it works:** Negotiations stall or fail when critical information remains hidden. Black Swans are the unknown unknowns -- secret constraints, hidden motivations, or unknown context -- that explain seemingly irrational behavior. Discovering even one can turn a stalemate into a breakthrough.

**Key insights:**
- Every negotiation has approximately 3 Black Swans lurking
- Types: secret constraints (boss said no more than X), hidden motivations (need this deal to save their job), unknown context (competitor just made a move)
- Stay curious; ask calibrated questions to surface them
- Watch for anomalies -- odd reactions, hesitations, or inconsistencies signal hidden factors
- Listen in unguarded moments (before/after meetings, casual conversation)
- If they seem irrational, diagnose: are they (1) ill-informed, (2) constrained, or (3) hiding something?
- Use three types of leverage -- positive (what they want), negative (what they fear), and normative (their own stated values) -- to surface hidden information

**Product applications:**

| Context | Application | Example |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| **Enterprise sales** | Discover hidden budget or timeline constraints | "What happens internally if this doesn't get resolved by Q3?" |
| **Churn analysis** | Uncover the real reason behind cancellation | "It seems like something changed -- what happened?" |
| **Partnership deals** | Surface unstated strategic motivations | "How does this fit into your bigger picture for next year?" |
| **Hiring negotiations** | Discover candidate's true priorities | "What would make you regret not taking this offer?" |
| **Competitive situations** | Reveal information about competing offers | "It sounds like you're weighing other options -- what would help you decide?" |

**Copy patterns:**
- "What's the biggest thing I'm missing here?"
- "What would change everything about this situation?"
- "Help me understand -- what's really driving this?"
- "If there's something I should know that would make this work better for both of us..."

**Ethical boundary:** Hunt for Black Swans to create better outcomes for both sides, not to exploit private or sensitive information.

See: [references/techniques.md](references/techniques.md)

## Handling Common Situations

**They say "That's not fair":**
- Stop immediately: "I want to be fair. Have I done something unfair? Let's discuss it."

**They anchor with an extreme number:**
- Don't counter immediately; use calibrated questions: "How did you arrive at that figure?"

**They stop responding:**
- Send: "Have you given up on [the project]?" -- triggers "No" response

**They seem irrational:**
- Diagnose: Are they (1) ill-informed, (2) constrained, or (3) hiding something?
- Use calibrated questions to uncover which

## Counterpart Styles

Adapt your approach based on their negotiation style:

| Style | Signs | Adapt by... |
|-------|-------|-------------|
| **Analyst** | Methodical, data-driven, hates surprises | Use facts, be patient, don't rush |
| **Accommodator** | Friendly, relationship-focused, avoids conflict | Build rapport, but pin down specifics |
| **Assertive** | Direct, time-conscious, wants to win | Be efficient, stand firm, acknowledge their points |

**Insight:** Great negotiators borrow from all three styles as needed.

## Voice and Delivery

- **Default voice**: Positive, warm, light-hearted (with a smile)
- **Critical moments**: Late-Night DJ Voice -- slow, calm, downward inflection
- **After key statements**: Pause 4+ seconds (tactical silence)
- **Watch their nonverbals**: 7% words, 38% tone, 55% body language

## Common Mistakes

| Mistake | Why It Fails | Fix |
|---------|-------------|------|
| **Splitting the difference** | Creates lukewarm outcomes nobody is happy with | Hold firm; no deal is better than a bad deal |
| **Pushing for "yes"** | Makes people defensive and produces counterfeit agreement | Pursue "That's right" instead; let them say "No" first |
| **Arguing your position** | Triggers resistance and shuts down listening | Use calibrated questions and labels to let them talk |
| **Skipping the accusation audit** | Unaddressed objections fester and derail later | List every negative they might think; address them early |
| **Countering an extreme anchor immediately** | Validates their anchor as the starting point | Mirror, label, ask "How did you arrive at that?" |
| **Using "Why?"** | Sounds accusatory and triggers defensiveness | Replace with "What" and "How" questions |
| **Treating "You're right" as success** | It means they want you to go away, not that they agree | Keep working toward "That's right" |

## Quick Diagnostic

Audit any negotiation preparation or live interaction:

| Question | If No | Action |
|----------|-------|--------|
| Have I prepared an accusation audit? | Objections will blindside you | List every negative they might think; address early |
| Do I have 3-5 calibrated questions ready? | You'll default to arguing | Draft "How" and "What" questions targeting their needs |
| Have I identified my BATNA? | You'll accept a bad deal under pressure | Define your walk-away point before negotiating |
| Am I aiming for "That's right"? | You'll chase counterfeit "yes" | Summarize their position until they affirm genuinely |
| Have I considered their negotiation style? | One-size-fits-all approach will misfire | Assess if they're Analyst, Accommodator, or Assertive |
| Am I hunting for Black Swans? | You'll miss game-changing information | Stay curious; watch for anomalies; ask about the unexpected |

## Reference Files

- [techniques.md](references/techniques.md): Complete technique breakdowns including mirroring, labeling, tactical empathy, voice control, accusation audit, calibrated questions, no-oriented questions, "That's right", Ackerman method, Black Swans, counterpart styles, body language, and pronoun analysis with examples and psychological triggers

## Further Reading

This skill is based on Chris Voss's research and experience as an FBI hostage negotiator. For the complete methodology, case studies, and advanced techniques:

- [*"Never Split the Difference: Negotiating As If Your Life Depended On It"*](https://www.amazon.com/[REDACTED_TOKEN]/dp/[REDACTED_PHONE]?tag=wondelai00-20) by Chris Voss with Tahl Raz
- [*"The Full Fee Agent: How to Stack the Odds in Your Favor as a Real Estate Professional"*](https://www.amazon.com/Full-Fee-Agent-Stack-Professional/dp/[REDACTED_PHONE]?tag=wondelai00-20) by Chris Voss and Steve Shull (applying Voss's negotiation principles to real estate and professional services)

## About the Author

**Chris Voss** is a former FBI lead international kidnapping negotiator and the founder of the Black Swan Group, a consulting firm that trains businesses in tactical negotiation. During his 24-year career at the FBI, Voss was the Bureau's lead negotiator for international kidnapping cases and worked more than 150 hostage situations worldwide. He has taught negotiation at Harvard Law School, Georgetown University's McDonough School of Business, and MIT's Sloan School of Management. *Never Split the Difference* has become one of the most widely recommended business books in the world and has been translated into dozens of languages. Voss's methods are now used by Fortune 500 companies, law enforcement agencies, and professionals across every industry.

# Paraguay Market Research Skill (v2.0)

Manual deep-dive market research for any business vertical in Paraguay.
Use this when search engines block automated queries (Google CAPTCHA, DuckDuckGo
bot detection, Cloudflare). Combines multiple free data sources into structured
lead lists with phone numbers, addresses, and website status.

## When to Use This Skill

- User asks to research a market, find clients, analyze competition
- User wants "all companies" or "all businesses" in a sector
- User wants to understand "who builds" or "who buys" something in Paraguay
- User asks for reseller / distribution / channel opportunities
- Any request involving `.com.py` domains or Paraguayan construction/glass industry

## Execution Pipeline (strict order)

### Phase 0: Understand Scope (first, always)

Before any data collection, clarify:
- What exactly is being sold? (product / service / material)
- Who is the end client? (construction companies? glass shops? hardware stores?)
- Are they looking for DIRECT customers or CHANNEL PARTNERS (resellers)?
- Should the output be a git repo or just a file?
- What tier structure to use for leads? (buyers vs influencers vs distributors)

This prevents wasted effort collecting the wrong type of lead.

### Phase 1: Landscape Assessment

1. **Wikipedia API** (always available, no bot blocking):
   - `curl -sL 'https://en.wikipedia.org/w/api.php?action=parse&page=<PAGE>&format=json&prop=text&origin=*'`
   - Extract tables: find `<table[^>]*class=.wikitable[^>]*>` then parse `<tr>` + `<td>` with regex
   - Best for: building/construction lists, company lists, industry overviews
   - Example: `List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Paraguay` returns all 27 towers with heights, floors, years

2. **DNS reconnaissance** (batch check 30+ domains at once):
   ```python
   import subprocess
   for domain in domains:
       r = subprocess.run(["host", domain], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
       ips = [line for line in r.stdout.split('\n') if 'has address' in line]
       status = "OK: "+ips[0].split()[-1] if ips else "NXDOMAIN"
   ```
   - Check BOTH `.com.py` and `.com` variants
   - Batch 20-30 at a time, write to execute_code
   - 80-90% of .com.py domains will NOT resolve — that's normal for PY
   - DNS = NXDOMAIN means no hosted website exists (some have Facebook/Instagram only)
   - DNS = OK means there IS a website to visit for contact info

3. **Company website visits** (for DNS-resolved domains):
   - Visit each via browser_navigate
   - Extract: phone, email, address, services/products, key personnel, contact form
   - Look in footer, "Contacto" page, "Nosotros" page
   - Check for WhatsApp link — many PY businesses use it as primary contact
   - If SSL error, try `http://` (non-SSL) or just note the cert issue

### Phase 2: Deep Discovery (Google Maps Method)

4. **Google Maps search** (the goldmine when all search engines are blocked):

   Navigate to: `https://www.google.com/maps/search/<query>`
   Then use `browser_snapshot` to read the results panel from the DOM.

   **Critical: The snapshot IS the data** — the Canvas map element is visual-only.
   All text (business names, phones, addresses, ratings, website links) is in the
   `<feed>` element's `<article>` children in the DOM.

   **Query strategy** — run 3-5 different queries per vertical. Each query returns
   ~7-8 results. Different queries = different sets of companies:
   - `[category] Asuncion Paraguay` — general
   - `[product/detail] Asuncion Paraguay` — specific
   - `[service type] Asuncion Paraguay` — service-focused

   **To run a new search**: Find the combobox in the snapshot (it shows the current
   search text), use `browser_type` with the element ref to type the new query,
   then `browser_press` with key="Enter", then `browser_snapshot` to get results.

   **Data extraction from snapshot**: Each `<article>` contains:
   - Business name (in a `<link>` element)
   - Star rating (as an image, check ref=e*'s alt text like "4,5 estrelas")
   - Address / description text
   - Phone number (typically in a generic/StaticText block like "Fechado · Abre ... +595 XXX XXX XXX")
   - Website link (may say "Acessar o site de [Company]")
   - Routing button

   **Important: Each query returns DIFFERENT companies.** Results are limited to ~7-8
   per query. Running the same query again gets the same results. To find more
   companies, run MORE queries with different search terms — not the same one repeatedly.

   **Query templates by vertical**:

   | Vertical | Queries to try |
   |----------|---------------|
   | Construction | `constructora civil Asuncion Paraguay`, `constructora edificio Asuncion`, `constructora torre Asuncion` |
   | Glass/Facades | `vidrio templado laminado fachada Asuncion`, `fachada vidrio aluminio Asuncion Paraguay`, `vidrio construccion Asuncion Paraguay`, `blindex Paraguay` |
   | Architecture | `arquitecto Asuncion Paraguay fachada`, `estudio arquitectura Asuncion` |
   | Development | `desarrolladora inmobiliaria Asuncion Paraguay`, `constructora edificio torre Asuncion` |
   | Hardware | `ferreteria construccion Asuncion`, `materiales construccion Asuncion` |
   | Aluminum | `carpinteria aluminio Asuncion`, `aberturas aluminio Asuncion` |

   **Pitfall**: Only ~7-8 results per query. To get more companies, run MORE
   queries (vary the search terms), not the same query repeatedly.

### Phase 3: Compilation & Deliverable

5. **Build the deliverable** as a git repo at `/root/<project-name>/`:

   ```
   project-name/
   ├── README.md
   ├── data/
   │   └── QUICK_REFERENCE.md     # Phone cheat sheet, tier lookup
   └── docs/
       ├── RESEARCH_SOURCES.md     # Methodology
       ├── 01_market_analysis/
       │   └── MARKET_ANALYSIS.md   # Market size, opportunities, trends
       ├── 02_customer_needs/
       │   └── CUSTOMER_NEEDS.md    # Per buyer type: pain points, value props
       ├── 03_competitors/
       │   └── COMPETITOR_ANALYSIS.md # Competitive landscape
       ├── 04_lead_database/
       │   └── LEAD_DATABASE.md     # Full leads with phone/email/address/tier
       └── 05_action_plan/
           └── ACTION_PLAN.md       # Prioritized outreach with call scripts
   ```

   **Tier system for leads**:
   - **Tier 1**: Direct buyers (companies that buy/install your product)
   - **Tier 2**: Channel partners (could resell or distribute)
   - **Tier 3**: Influencers (architects, engineers who specify)
   - **Tier 4**: End clients (developers, contractors who need the product)
   - **Tier 5**: Retail/distribution

   For each lead: name, website status (✅/❌), phone, email, address, rating,
   products/services, tier assignment, priority call order.

   Include a **phone cheat sheet** at the top of the lead database with all
   numbers sorted by priority.

   ```bash
   git init && git add -A && git commit -m "Initial commit: ..."
   ```

6. **Push to GitHub** under the Ai-Whisperers org:
   ```bash
   gh repo create Ai-Whisperers/<repo-name> --private --description "..."
   git remote add origin https://github.com/Ai-Whisperers/<repo-name>.git
   git push -u origin main
   ```

## Pitfalls & Lessons Learned

### Search Engine Blocks (This Will Happen)

- **Google**: CAPTCHA on every automated search from non-PY IPs
- **DuckDuckGo**: CAPTCHA + "select the duck" image challenges on lite.duckduckgo.com
- **Bing, Brave**: Same — all block
- **SearXNG**: Install locally if available; try `localhost:8888` or `localhost:4000`
- **Workaround**: Google Maps front-end via browser (not search API)

### Paraguayan Company Web Presence

- **80-90% of .com.py domains don't resolve** — this is normal
- Many companies operate via Facebook/Instagram/WhatsApp only
- DNS = NXDOMAIN does NOT mean the company doesn't exist
- Some DNS-resolved domains have SSL errors or timeout — still valid businesses

### Google Maps Pitfalls

- **Limited view**: only ~7-8 results per query, logged-in view has more
- **No emails**: emails rarely appear in the limited view — visit company website
- **Phone numbers are reliable** but may reach a retail storefront
- **Portuguese interface**: Maps defaults to Portuguese for Brazil-region IPs, but data is for Paraguay
- **Vision analysis fails**: browser_vision with image_url mode errors on some models. Rely on DOM snapshot.
- **LinkedIn blocks without login**: company pages show auth wall
- **News sites**: search results on ultimahora.com/abc.com.py return 10+ news stories, not structured data
- **Business directories** (Infobel, Cybo, Paginas Amarillas PY): all blocked by Cloudflare

### Repo & Deliverable Pitfalls

- **README.md should clarify what "client" means** — there can be confusion between the end customer and the repo's audience. Be explicit.
- **Paraguay specific**: phone numbers in Paraguay format (+595 XXX XXX XXX), addresses with "casi" (corner of), barrio names
- **Include a note about research limitations** — any blocked sources, data freshness, unverified phone numbers
- **The paragu-ai-leads repo exists** (beauty/wellness, 7,463 businesses) — check it early to avoid duplicating work, but note it's a different vertical
- **Landing page / mobile-first design**: most PY businesses browse on WhatsApp Web, not desktop

## Spanish/Portuguese Terms Reference

| Glass shop | Vidrieria | Vidracaria |
| Hardware store | Ferreteria | Loja de ferramentas |
| Construction | Construccion | Construcao/Construtora |
| Facade | Fachada | Fachada |
| Curtain wall | Muro cortina | Muro cortina |
| Tempered glass | Vidrio templado | Vidro temperado |
| Laminated glass | Vidrio laminado | Vidro laminado |
| Developer | Desarrolladora | Desenvolvedora |
| Contractor | Constructora | Construtora |
| Rating (stars) | Estrellas | Estrelas |
| Website link | Sitio web | "Acessar o site de" |
| Open/Closed | Abierto/Cerrado | Aberto/Fechado |
| Phone | Telefono | Telefone |
| Route | Ruta | Rotas |
| Search | Buscar | Pesquisar |

## Post-Research: Website Planning (if user wants a site built)

After market research is complete, the user may ask to build a website for the
client. This is a **different class of site** from our B2C e-commerce template:

### B2B Lead Gen vs B2C E-commerce — Critical Differences

| Dimension | B2C (Fun4Me, Superspuma, depiflash) | B2B (construction glass, law firms) |
|-----------|--------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Goal | Sell products online | Generate sales leads |
| Content | Product photos, prices | Technical specs, certifications, portfolio |
| Pricing | Display prices | "Request quote" — every project is custom |
| Trust signals | Reviews, ratings | Project portfolio, client logos, certs |
| CTA | "Buy now" / "Add to cart" | "Solicitar cotización" / WhatsApp |
| SEO keywords | Product names | "muro cortina Paraguay" (industry terms) |
| Decision cycle | Minutes | Weeks (projects are planned) |
| Theme | Dark, brand-forward | Clean, professional, industry-appropriate (blue for construction) |
| Feature priority | Cart, checkout, payments | Contact form, quote request, technical data |

### When the user asks "make a website for this client":

1. **Clarify**: Is this B2B or B2C? If B2B, the template-nextjs-client needs
   SIGNIFICANT modifications — don't just clone and change colors.

2. **Site architecture for B2B lead gen** (standard 7-page structure):
   ```
   /                 → Home (hero + product overview + trust signals + CTA)
   /nosotros         → About (company, team, certifications, numbers)
   /productos        → Products (technical specs WITHOUT prices, "request quote")
   /proyectos        → Portfolio (gallery of completed installations)
   /servicios        → Services (consulting, supply, installation, after-sales)
   /blog             → Blog (SEO content targeting zero-competition keywords)
   /contacto         → Contact (form + WhatsApp + phone + map)
   /cotizar          → Quote request (standalone form for campaign links)
   ```

3. **Content strategy** — every piece of content should answer:
   - What do you sell? (glass products with technical data)
   - Why should they trust you? (certifications, portfolio, experience)
   - How do they buy? (call or form, not cart)
   - What does it cost? (depends on project — request a quote)

4. **SEO advantage**: Paraguay's B2B market has ZERO companies doing SEO for
   industry keywords. A simple blog with 3-5 articles can dominate:
   - "muro cortina Paraguay"
   - "vidrio fachada Asunción"
   - "precio vidrio templado Paraguay"
   - "blindex Paraguay"

5. **Theme**: Use blue/white for construction (trust, professionalism, safety).
   Don't use the dark theme from the template — it looks wrong for B2B.

6. **No prices visible** — every project is custom. Use "Solicitar cotización"
   as the primary CTA. The contact/quote form replaces the cart.

## Verification Checklist

- [ ] Scope clarified (direct buyer vs channel vs influencer?)
- [ ] Wikipedia API scraped for relevant pages
- [ ] DNS batch check on 20+ domain variants
- [ ] Working company websites visited for contact info
- [ ] Google Maps searched with 3-5 different queries
- [ ] All found companies documented with phone + rating
- [ ] For DNS-resolved domains: websites visited and contact info extracted
- [ ] Lead tiers assigned
- [ ] Deliverable structured as a git repo
- [ ] Phone cheat sheet included
- [ ] Research sources / limitations documented
- [ ] Repo pushed to Ai-Whisperers GitHub org
- [ ] If user also wants a site: determined if B2B or B2C (they're built differently; see `references/b2b-construction-website-plan.md`)

function.arguments:
{"action": "create", "name": "client-sales-pitch", "category": "management", "content": "---
name: client-sales-pitch
description: >-
  Build a named-prospect sales pitch for a Paraguayan business lead.
  Research the prospect's business (repo content, live site, CLAUDE.md),
  profile the owner/decision-maker, and produce a structured pitch document
  with sections: approach, pain, solution, differentiator, offer, exchange,
  objections, follow-up scenarios. Designed for Ai-Whisperers / Paragu-IA's
  first-client acquisition strategy and ongoing sales pipeline.
version: 1.0.0
author: Erebus — Ai-Whisperers
license: MIT
triggers:
  - "sales pitch"
  - "pitch for"
  - "proposal for"
  - "venderle a"
  - "client pitch"
  - "primer cliente"
  - "first client"
  - "named prospect"
  - "presentar a"
  - "guion de ventas para"
  - "pitch personalizado"
  - "pitch especifico"
  - "cerrar a"
  - "contactar a"
---

# Client Sales Pitch

Build a complete, named-prospect sales pitch for a Paraguayan business lead. This skill covers the full process: research the prospect's business, profile the decision-maker, craft the pitch document, and handle follow-up.

## Pipeline

```
Lead identified → Business research → Prospect profiling → Pitch document → Review → Deploy
```

## Phase 1: Business Research

Before writing a single word of the pitch, understand the business deeply:

1. **Read the live site** — browse via browser_navigate, note:
   - Products/catalog size and categories
   - Pricing display (or lack thereof)
   - Brand voice and visuals
   - Existing CTAs (WhatsApp, phone, contact form)
   - About page (history, team, location)

2. **Read the repo documents** — check for:
   - `CLAUDE.md` — agent guide, project overview, team roles
   - `content/es.json` (or equivalent) — all product data, FAQ, pages
   - `docs/` directory — brand guides, client questionnaires, architecture docs
   - `README.md` — project purpose, tech stack

3. **Extract business intelligence** from content:
   - Location (city, neighborhood, street if available)
   - Product count and categories
   - Current sales channels (WhatsApp, local, social media)
   - Any existing online presence (website, Facebook, Instagram)
   - Pricing patterns (fixed prices vs request-quote)

4. **Check the live site via browser** — confirm:
   - Site is working and accessible
   - Product catalog is complete
   - WhatsApp integration is functional
   - Mobile responsiveness (Paraguay is mobile-first)

## Phase 2: Prospect Profiling

Gather information about the person you're pitching to:

- **Name and role:** Owner? Manager? Decision-maker?
- **Relationship to team:** How did this lead come in? (referral, cold outreach, existing contact?)
- **Pain points to address:** What specific problems does their current setup have?
- **Motivation:** Why would they say yes? (growth, modernization, competition, convenience?)
- **Objections to anticipate:** What will they push back on? (price, tech skills, time, trust?)

If the user knows the prospect already, ask directly for:
- Their personality style (direct? analytical? relationship-first?)
- Any previous conversations or context
- Their biggest business concern right now

## Phase 3: Pitch Document Structure

Create the pitch at `/root/paragu-ai-leads/docs/05_strategy/PITCH_<BUSINESS>_<OWNER>.md`

Structure:

```markdown
# Pitch de Ventas — [Owner Name] / [Business Name]

*Cliente: [context — e.g., Primer caso de éxito e-commerce de Paragu-AI]*

---

## Perfil del Cliente

**Negocio:** [Description]
**Ubicación:** [City, neighborhood]
**Productos:** [Count + categories]
**WhatsApp:** [Primary sales channel]
**Presencia actual:** [Local + WhatsApp + social media]

---

## Pitch Inicial (WhatsApp)

Short first-contact message. Natural, not salesy. Opens with genuine interest.

---

## Pitch Completo (llamada o presencial)

**Apertura** — Recognize their situation. "El Viajero tiene un local en Mariano Roque Alonso y vende por WhatsApp, ¿cierto?"

**El Dolor** — 3-5 specific pains of their current setup. Concrete, not generic.

**La Solución** — What we built/delivered. How it works step by step.

**El Diferenciador** — Why this is different/better. "La web no reemplaza tu WhatsApp — lo potencia."

**La Oferta** — First-client pricing or special terms. Be specific.

**El Intercambio** — What we ask in return (testimonial, video, referral pipeline).

**Cierre** — Specific call to action with next step.

---

## Objeciones esperadas y respuestas

List 3-5 objections with concrete responses. Common ones:

- "Ya tengo Facebook/Instagram"
- "No sé manejar eso"
- "Cuánto tiempo toma"
- "Y si no funciona"
- "Por qué tan barato los primeros meses?"

Each objection gets: the exact objection + a response that validates + reframes + solves.

---

## URLs a mostrar

List of live URLs to show during the pitch. These are concrete proof.

**WhatsApp demo note:** Show how clicking a product opens WhatsApp with product info preloaded.

---

## Seguimiento

3 scenarios with exact scripts:

- **Si dice que sí:** Next steps, timeline, what to expect
- **Si dice "lo voy a pensar":** Gentle urgency + space to decide
- **Si dice que no:** Graceful exit with door open
```

## Phase 4: First-Client Offer Template

When this is the first e-commerce client (1000+ products), use the confirmed marketing technique:

```
Setup: 0 Gs
3 meses completamente gratis
Mes 4 en adelante: 639.000 Gs/mes (Plan Empresa)

A cambio:
1. Testimonio honesto
2. Video de 30 segundos
3. Caso de estudio (sin costo para ellos)
```

The pitch hook: "Tu tienda online funcionando sin pagar nada hasta el mes 4."

## Price Reference (Fixed)

These are the CONFIRMED prices. Do not change them:

| Plan | Setup | Monthly | Annual |
|------|-------|---------|--------|
| Solo Hosting | 450K Gs | 97K Gs | 970K Gs |
| Básico | 450K Gs | 187K Gs | 1,935K Gs |
| Profesional | 450K Gs | 381K Gs | 3,870K Gs |
| Empresa | 450K Gs | 639K Gs | 6,450K Gs |

Setup is FREE if annual is paid upfront.

## WhatsApp Output Rules

When presenting the pitch to the user on WhatsApp:
- NO markdown tables
- Plain text lists with clear labels
- Guarani prices first, USD in parentheses
- One scenario/layout at a time, not all options
- Keep descriptions to 1 sentence per item

## Pitfalls

- **Don't write the pitch in English — the user and prospect are Spanish speakers.** Write the pitch document in Spanish.
- **Don't guess business details.** Read the actual content files and live site. Every detail in the pitch must be verifiable.
- **Don't skip the objection section.** The user will use it in live conversation. 3-5 objections minimum.
- **Don't genericize the pitch.** It must feel specific to THIS business, not copy-paste from another pitch.
- **Don't forget the "intercambio" section.** The user needs to know what to ask for in return for the first-client discount.
- **The "48-hour" hook is your strongest card.** Lead with it when presenting time-to-value.
- **When presenting to the user, send the pitch file reference, not the full content in WhatsApp.**
- **The Paragua-IA docs live at /root/paragu-ai-leads/docs/05_strategy/**

## Verification

- [ ] Business researched via live site + repo content
- [ ] Prospect profile collected or asked about
- [ ] Pitch document saved to /root/paragu-ai-leads/docs/05_strategy/
- [ ] All sections present: perfil, approach, pain, solution, differentiator, offer, exchange, close
- [ ] 3-5 objections with specific responses
- [ ] 3 follow-up scenarios
- [ ] First-client offer included if applicable
- [ ] File referenced to user on WhatsApp (not full content pasted)
- [ ] Spanish language throughout

## References

- The source pricing documents live in `/root/paragu-ai-leads/docs/05_strategy/`
- Example pitch: `PITCH_EL_VIAJERO_OSMAR.md` (real named-prospect pitch used in session)
- `client-pricing-proposals` skill for detailed pricing breakdowns
- `negotiation` skill for objection handling techniques if the user needs deeper tactics
"}

## Key details from this pitch

- **Business type:** Camping, fishing, auto/moto accessories (retail e-commerce)
- **Location:** Coronel Felipe Toledo, Mariano Roque Alonso
- **Product count:** 200+ products, 7 categories
- **Primary channel:** WhatsApp sales
- **Existing presence:** Physical store + WhatsApp (+ possible Facebook/Instagram)
- **First-client offer used:** 3 months free + 0 setup

## Structure demonstrated

1. Perfil del Cliente (business snapshot for the seller to reference)
2. Pitch Inicial (WhatsApp — short, first contact)
3. Pitch Completo (call/presencial — pain, solution, differentiator, offer, exchange, close)
4. 5 objections with responses
5. 3 follow-up scenarios (yes, thinking, no)

## What made this pitch work

- Specifics about the business (camping, Mariano Roque Alonso, 200+ products)
- Direct reference to their actual pain (answering same questions 20x/day)
- The "web doesn't replace WhatsApp, it powers it" reframe
- Concrete exchange ask (testimonial + 30s video + case study)
- First-client urgency without pressure
"}
